patient_information.allergies.answer: Allopurinol – developed a diffuse pruritic maculopapular rash and mild facial swelling within 10 days of initiation in 2019; medication discontinued and documented as drug allergy. Colchicine – significant gastrointestinal intolerance (severe diarrhea and abdominal cramping) even at low doses; not anaphylactic but poorly tolerated, so avoided except for very short courses if absolutely necessary. Penicillin – reported childhood rash and hives after penicillin; has not been rechallenged as an adult, listed as allergy for safety. Seasonal/environmental allergies – mild nasal congestion and itchy eyes in spring; controlled with over-the-counter antihistamines. No known reactions to prior IV infusions or contrast dyes.

krystexxa_order_details.krystexxa_order.answer: KRYSTEXXA (pegloticase) 8 mg IV every 2 weeks for uncontrolled chronic gout with tophaceous deposits. Infuse 8 mg in 250 mL normal saline over no less than 120 minutes via peripheral or central IV line using an in-line 0.2–1.2 micron protein-sparing filter. Premedicate prior to each infusion with: (1) oral antihistamine (e.g., cetirizine 10 mg PO or equivalent) 60 minutes before infusion, (2) acetaminophen 650–1000 mg PO 30–60 minutes before infusion, and (3) corticosteroid – methylprednisolone 125 mg IV 30 minutes before infusion (or equivalent per protocol) to reduce risk of infusion reactions. Maintain patient on urate-lowering prophylaxis per referring rheumatologist’s instructions (no xanthine oxidase inhibitors while on KRYSTEXXA; continue low-dose prednisone 5 mg daily and intermittent low-dose colchicine only if tolerated). Monitor for signs/symptoms of anaphylaxis and infusion reactions throughout and for at least 1 hour after infusion; have emergency medications and equipment available (epinephrine, H1/H2 blockers, corticosteroids, airway support). Hold or discontinue therapy and notify ordering physician if pre-infusion serum uric acid level rises above 6.0 mg/dL on two consecutive measurements, or if the patient develops any serious hypersensitivity or infusion reaction. Adjust or discontinue per rheumatologist’s guidance if any significant change in renal function, severe anemia, or other concerning lab abnormalities.

standing_lab_orders.frequency.answer: Obtain CBC and CMP within 7 days prior to first KRYSTEXXA infusion, then every 8 weeks while on therapy, or more frequently if clinically indicated. Draw serum uric acid level immediately prior to each infusion; if pre-infusion uric acid is >6.0 mg/dL on two consecutive occasions, hold further infusions and notify Dr. Bennett for reassessment.

notes_and_location.notes_additional_comm.answer: Patient is a 62-year-old male with a 15+ year history of gout, now with chronic tophaceous gout involving bilateral first MTP joints, ankles, and olecranon bursae. He has failed or not tolerated standard urate-lowering therapies including allopurinol (allergic rash and facial swelling) and febuxostat (inadequate uric acid control despite maximized dosing, with persistent flares and tophi). Colchicine causes significant GI side effects but can sometimes be used for very short courses; currently he is on low-dose prednisone 5 mg daily for flare prevention. Baseline serum uric acid prior to KRYSTEXXA consideration was 10.2 mg/dL (measured 2025-11-20). G6PD testing was completed on 2025-11-15 and was normal; documentation is included. He has no history of anaphylaxis but does have a history of medication rashes, so please adhere closely to premedication protocol and monitor carefully during and after the infusion. He works as a senior HVAC technician and spends long hours on his feet, which has become increasingly difficult due to chronic foot and ankle pain from gout. He reports difficulty sleeping due to nighttime pain and stiffness, and he has had three flares in the last six months requiring urgent care visits and steroid tapers. Transportation: patient drives himself and can reliably attend every-2-week infusions; prefers early morning appointments (between 8:00–10:00 AM) on Tuesdays or Thursdays due to work schedule. Primary language is English; no interpreter required. Emergency contact: wife, Karen Harris, cell (918) 555-6032. Please call Sarah Collins, RN at the rheumatology clinic if there are any concerns about infusion reactions, lab abnormalities, or missed appointments. Patient has been counseled extensively on potential risks of KRYSTEXXA (including infusion reactions, anaphylaxis, and possible cardiovascular risks) and has signed informed consent at the rheumatology office; a copy of the signed consent and detailed clinic notes are included with this referral.
